EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening area of low pressure moved out of the Gulf of Mexico, across southern Georgia, and then up the southeast coast. As the low passed south of the region, light snow became heavier across the mountains of extreme northern Greenville County. The heavy snow continued throughout the afternoon. Snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour became common across the area during the afternoon. The heavy, wet snow combined with gusty winds to cause numerous trees and power lines to fall across the area during the afternoon. Power outages resulted, and some customers were without power for as much as a week. The snow quickly ended on the evening of the 18th. Total accumulations ranged from 6-10 inches.||Many traffic accidents were reported during the storm, and continued for several days thereafter, as continuous melting and refreezing of ice and snow resulted in treacherous road conditions during the late night and morning hours.

After a period of heavy rain that left the ground saturated, strong winds developed behind a cold front during the late morning hours over the Sorth Carolina mountains. The combination of very windy conditions and wet ground resulted in numerous fallen trees, which brought down power lines and damaged homes and cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms moved across the Midlands and Pee Dee taking down trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported powerlines down on Broad River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked east across the Carolinas, then pushed offshore, dumping two to four inches of snow across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began falling the morning of January 20th, and spotters in McColl reported two inches of accumulation by mid morning, with around an inch reported in Bennettsville. Many businesses were closed, and all schools were closed. No weather related traffic accidents were relayed to the National Weather Service.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain saturated soils across the area and wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph toppled a few trees in the Midlands.  A few isolated thunderstorms also took down some trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported a few trees down on powerlines causing outages around Saluda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across much of Upstate South Carolina during the late afternoon. The snow became heavy at times, particularly along the I-85 corridor. By mid-evening, 1-4 inches of accumulation was reported across much of the area. Snow, heavy at times and accompanied by occasional lightning, continued into the evening hours. By the time the snow tapered off, accumulations of 3-6��� were common across the area. However, localized amounts of up to 8 inches were reported along the I-85 corridor.  The heavy wet snow, combined with gusty winds, caused quite a few trees and power lines to fall, resulting in numerous power outages. Some customers were without power for several days. One tree fell on and damaged a home in Gaffney. In addition, there were numerous traffic accidents.

A strong cold front along with a vigorous upper level storm system tracked through the southeast United States, resulting in numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The media relayed a report of nickel size hail was off Otranto Road in North Charleston, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms produced microbursts and hail in the central and upper Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported many trees down on homes and 3/4 inch hail with a microburst that hit the Lancaster area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP observer reported 3/4 inch hail for a couple of minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms moved across the lower midlands and produced large tornadoes.  Other storms in the central midlands produced large hail and microburst wind damage.  The largest tornado tracked across Aiken county along highway 278.  There was one indirect fatality and around a dozen people were sent to the hospital. Several homes were destroyed and many had moderate to severe damage.  Widespread trees and powerlines were also down in Aiken county.  Total damage estimate was 6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SKYWARN spotter reported 1 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly unstable air-mass with steep lapse rates below 600 mb was lifted by the sea breeze. Lift was also provided by the Piedmont/ Sandhills trough which allowed convection to develop during the early to mid afternoon. By late afternoon and evening, numerous boundary collisions produced severe pulse storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail about the size of quarters was reported along SC-9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong easterly swells contributed to a rip related death.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An east wind and a strong easterly swell caused strong rip currents to occur near Myrtle Beach. A woman swimming near the 68th Avenue North beach access got caught in a rip current and drowned.

Total accumulations ranged from trace amounts in the lower elevations near the Blue Ridge, to 3 inches or more in the higher elevations.

Strong winds developed around noon along the I-85 corridor and persisted through the afternoon. Numerous trees were blown onto roads and power lines, resulting in quite a few power outages across the area. Some trees  fell on automobiles, including one near Spartanburg, which caused the occupants to require rescue. A large tree fell across two homes in Spartanburg, causing significant roof damage. The wind also caused minor roof damage to the town hall in Blacksburg, SC. A couple of homes were also damaged by falling trees in the Seneca area. Wet ground resulting from heavy rain during the preceding days likely contributed to the tree damage.

Several homes were destroyed and many had moderate to severe damage.  Widespread trees and powerlines were also down in Aiken county.  Total damage estimate was 6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported nickel size hail near hwy 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms moved into the western upstate in the early evening hours. As the evening progressed, the supercells tended to evolve into a series of bow echoes. Tornadoes were spawned by the supercells and the resultant bow echoes. There was also quite a bit of large hail and straight-line wind damage in places as well. The most significant damage occurred in Greenwood and Abbeville Counties, though tornadoes also caused damage in parts of Anderson and Union Counties as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS survey found an area of tornado damage that began about 3 miles southwest of Jonesville in the Proctor/Zig Zag Rd area. Several trees were snapped off or uprooted in this area and some outdoor objects blown around. The tornado moved almost due east to T Bishop Rd, where a mobile home was destroyed and another heavily damaged. Dozens of trees were also uprooted and snapped off in this area. The tornado continued along an intermittent path to the east before uprooting numerous pine and cedar trees on Jonesville Highway about 3 miles southeast of Jonesville. A 3-ton trailer was moved about 100 feet, and a home received minor roof damage at this location. The tornado continued on to Bob Little Rd, where a plant building received major roof damage. The tornado then damaged two mobile homes before lifting 3.5 SE of Jonesville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began along Old Williamston Rd near its intersection with Brooks Rd. Tornadoes were spawned by the supercells and the resultant bow echoes. There was also quite a bit of large hail and straight-line wind damage in places as well. The most significant damage occurred in Greenwood and Abbeville Counties, though tornadoes also caused damage in parts of Anderson and Union Counties as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS survey found an area of tornado damage that began on Dry Rock Rd near its intersection with Andersonville Rd. (It is possible that the tornado began over Lake Hartwell.) Damage was mainly confined to uprooted or snapped trees at this point, although two mobile homes were shifted off their foundation at this location.. The most significant damage occurred just east of this location, in the Snyder Rd area. A storage shelter was destroyed in this area and a travel trailer blown off concretet blocks. Other storage shelters received signifiumerous trees were snapped off or uprooted along Snyder Rd, with trees falling on several homes. The tornado traveled east from Snyder Rd, crossing an inlet of Lake Hartwell before re-emerging in the Sandy Shores Dr area. The tornado continued east, moving back over Lake Hartwell, where some trees were blown down on islands. The tornado apparently dissipated over the lake. East of Snyder Rd, damage was almost exclusively confined to snapped and uprooted trees, although some boat shelters and docks on Lake Hartwell were lifted and tossed as much as 100 feet. This tornado was produced by a supercell thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms moved into the western upstate in the early evening hours. As the evening progressed, the supercells tended to evolve into a series of bow echoes. Tornadoes were spawned by the supercells and the resultant bow echoes. There was also quite a bit of large hail and straight-line wind damage in places as well. The most significant damage occurred in Greenwood and Abbeville Counties, though tornadoes also caused damage in parts of Anderson and Union Counties as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least two trees were blown down across roads in the northwest corner of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mini-supercell thunderstorm produced a weak tornado over the central Upstate of South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado path begin near the intersection of Amber Hill Cir and Watts Bridge Rd south of Cross Hill, where a few small trees were snapped off. The tornado moved east northeast to an RV park near the intersection of Watts Bridge and Morse Landing Rd. Two travel trailers were blown over and rolled 20-30 yards at this location. An outbuilding was destroyed and a mobile home received minor roof damage just south of the park. A few trees were snapped off in this area as well. The tornado appeared to lift as it crossed a hollow just east of Morse Landing Rd. However, additional damage to trees was observed on Barnett Rd. The tornado lifted near the intersection of Skippers Lodge Rd just north of Barnett Rd, where two large pine trees were uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms moved into the western upstate in the early evening hours. As the evening progressed, the supercells tended to evolve into a series of bow echoes. Tornadoes were spawned by the supercells and the resultant bow echoes. There was also quite a bit of large hail and straight-line wind damage in places as well. The most significant damage occurred in Greenwood and Abbeville Counties, though tornadoes also caused damage in parts of Anderson and Union Counties as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS survey identified a tornado path that began near the intersection of Hunters Creek Bvd and Woodlawn Rd. The tornado continued east/southeast across the south side of Greenwood, uprooting or snapping off numerous trees along the path. The hardest hit area was around Edgefield St, where numerous trees were damaged. Some trees fell on and seriously damaged homes. Other homes received significant roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning was the probable cause of a church fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the Garden City Baptist Church early Sunday morning June 28th, setting it on fire.  Half the church was burning, along with part of another building, before the fire fighters arrived. It took five hours to extinguish the blaze, and the church was destroyed.  A fireman injured his ankle in the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures reached the 90 degree mark which were well above normal for this time of year.  A recruit from Ft. Jackson died from heatstroke as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Ft. Jackson recruit died as a result of heatstroke.  Pvt. Jamal Britt, 19, of Edgewood, Md. died at 5:04pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced street flooding in the city of Florence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused street flooding on many roads in the city of Florence.  Route 76 and Palmetto Street were under a foot of water in many places. Other streets include: South Church at West Cheves, Brittany Drive, Pine and Elm at South Church Street, and South Dargan Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started a fire that destroyed a home in Murrells Inlet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house in the Blackmoor Golf Course subdivision.  The subsequent fire destroyed the home.  There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning destroyed a house in Jordanville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house off the Pee Dee Highway in Jordanville.  The house burned to the ground. No one was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the New England coast combined with a new moon produced high astronomical tides that caused minor coastal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unusually high tides produced flooding from 43rd to 45th Street between Marsh and Lake Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather affected western South Carolina as a line of storms moved across much of the region. Other severe storms also developed ahead of the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down over much of the western and central part of the county. Wind damage was most widespread from the GSP Airport, where the ASOS measured a wind gust of 76 mph, across Westgate Mall were numerous trees were blown down, to a few miles east of Spartanburg where trees were blown down on highway 56 at Wyatt Rd and Rushing Rd. At least one tree was blown onto a house in Spartanburg city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew across the area producing gusts to 50 mph.  The winds were associated with a strong low level jet that came through the area with a cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of saturated ground resulting from 3 to 5 inches of rain and gusty winds from the remnants of tropical storm Ida, and a developing cyclone off the Carolina coast, resulted in quite a few uprooted trees across the piedmont and foothills. Some trees fell on roads and power lines, resulting in periodic road closures and scattered power outages. Several homes and vehicles were damaged by falling trees across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A motorist was killed about 6 miles southeast of Anderson when he collided with a tree that had previously fallen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure in the lee of the Appalachians sparked thunderstorm development across central South Carolina which then propagated to the South Carolina coast.  The thunderstorms intensified as they met up with the sea breeze and collided with numerous outflow boundaries, resulting in scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team confirmed an EF-0 Tornado in Hanahan, South Carolina.  Based on eyewitness reports and a survey of the damage, an EF-0 Tornado developed over Turkey Creek and tracked south-southeast for about 0.25 miles along the marsh just to the east of east Recess Road and west of Dennis Drive before dissipating.  ||Straight-line winds produced extensive tree damage adjacent to the short path of the Tornado, along a 0.75 mile wide area extending from east Recess Road to Steward Street and along a length of approximately 0.70 miles extending from Turkey Creek to Meadowcliff Avenue.  Numerous large trees were knocked down, tops of many trees were sheared off and tops of some power poles were broken off.  There was minor damage to several homes and other structures, mainly a result of the fallen trees and tree limbs.  South Carolina Electric and Gas reported nearly 3500 customers were without power after the storm as a result of the downed power lines.  Along with the damaging winds, the public also reported quarter to golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed southward into the region and when combined with the sea breeze and an upper level disturbance, resulted in scattered thunderstorm development across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a tree down in the backyard of their residence along Lakeside Drive in Bluffton, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure in the lee of the Appalachians sparked thunderstorm development across central South Carolina which then propagated to the South Carolina coast.  The thunderstorms intensified as they met up with the sea breeze and collided with numerous outflow boundaries, resulting in scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down across Chisolm Road in Johns Island, South Carolina.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported several trees and large tree limbs down near Fields Farm along River Road in Johns Island, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure in the lee of the Appalachians sparked thunderstorm development across central South Carolina which then propagated to the South Carolina coast. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe convection developed along a lee side trough across the Midlands of South Carolina and central Georgia, then shifted east into the forecast area during the afternoon and evening as a potent upper level disturbance crossed the area. This convection interacted with the sea breeze (which happened to be pinched rather close to the coast) allowing for additional intensification closer to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Public reported at least penny size hail while driving home to the north end of Hilton Head Island, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance drove a squall line or Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) through southern South Carolina in the early morning hours. Another MCS pushed into the region and collided with the sea breeze in the evening, and resulted in scattered thunderstorm development across the area once again.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast Media relayed a public report of several large tree limbs down in a residential yard off of Highway 165, 1 mile north of Delemar Crossroads, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance drove a squall line or Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) through southern South Carolina in the early morning hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported a tree down on Coosaw Island, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low developed over the northern Gulf of Mexico Wednesday morning and intensified as it shifted northeast over South Carolina. As this process occurred, strong onshore flow allowed deep moisture to advect over the Southeast within the warm sector of the low pressure system. Strong onshore winds in combination with a morning high tide led to coastal flooding along the South Carolina coast through 730 am. Strong isentropic ascent ahead of the surface low then led to heavy shower development over South Carolina shortly after 730 am. At this time, all flooding reported was due to heavy rainfall over the area. A cold front then swept through the southeast Wednesday evening which helped produce several strong thunderstorms through early Wednesday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a road closure due to flooding along at Radcliffe Street and Coming Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced wind damage and torrential downpoors in the central Midlands.  Trees and powerlines came down and flash flooding occurred in downtown Columbia with water several feet in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media and public reported torrential rains causing flash flooding in east central Lexington and west central Richland counties.  Three to five inches of rain fell within a 1 to 3 hour period causing flash flooding in several areas, especially in down town Columbia.  Water levels rose to nearly 12 feet at the gage on Rocky Branch Creek near the intersection of Main and Whaley streets.  Several cars were submerged and flooding extended into the USC campus area.  Flooding in the Five-Points area and on Gervais street near Harden street had water up to the windows of cars and the foot bridge across Rocky Branch Creek at Maxcy Gregg Park was submerged.  Several people had to be rescued from trapped vehicles in the flooded areas of Columbia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the eastern Gulf of Mexico during the early afternoon hours. This system then shifted northeast over the Florida peninsula and then along the coast of South Carolina during the evening and early night hours. Saturated soils along with strong onshore winds and the timing of astronomical high tides created favorable conditions for flash flooding over southeast South Carolina. As forcing from the low pressure system met a weak warm front along the South Carolina coast, heavy rainfall developed. Rainfall accumulations ranging from 3 to 6 inches led to several road closures during astronomical high tide in and around areas surrounding Charleston, South Carolina. Charleston city law enforcement took 21 calls to rescue people stranded in vehicles and 3 calls to rescue people stranded at residences during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a road closure due to flooding along Halfway Creek Road between Steed Creek Road and Shulerville Road.

Strong isentropic ascent ahead of the surface low then led to heavy shower development over South Carolina shortly after 730 am. At this time, all flooding reported was due to heavy rainfall over the area. A cold front then swept through the southeast Wednesday evening which helped produce several strong thunderstorms through early Wednesday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire department reported one tree down on Foster Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low developed over the northern Gulf of Mexico Wednesday morning and intensified as it shifted northeast over South Carolina. As this process occurred, strong onshore flow allowed deep moisture to advect over the Southeast within the warm sector of the low pressure system. Strong onshore winds in combination with a morning high tide led to coastal flooding along the South Carolina coast through 730 am. Strong isentropic ascent ahead of the surface low then led to heavy shower development over South Carolina shortly after 730 am. At this time, all flooding reported was due to heavy rainfall over the area. A cold front then swept through the southeast Wednesday evening which helped produce several strong thunderstorms through early Wednesday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported knee deep water completely over Bee Street in downtown Charleston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving area of low pressure moved across the southern Appalachians during the afternoon of December 2nd. Widespread 3 to 4 inch rainfall totals were reported with the system, including 3.93 inches at the Anderson ASOS and 3.11 inches at the GSP ASOS. As the ground was already quite moist from several recent rain events, a few areas of flash flooding developed, particularly in the Greenville and Anderson metro areas. A small area of thunderstorms developed on the southern end of the rain shield. The storms produced some isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Urban and poor drainage flooding caused closure of a handful of roads in the northern half of Spartanburg County, including portions of Monks Grove Church Rd and highway 221 near Spartanburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure brought a period of moderate to heavy rain to the Upstate and northwest piedmont of South Carolina on Christmas day. Around 2 to 3 inches of rain fell over the northwest piedmont resulting in an area of flash flooding to the south of Rock Hill.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding developed at several locations in the southeast part of the county after heavy rain fell throughout the morning. The main problem area was along Wildcat Creek, where a stream gauge exceeded the established flood stage by 2 feet, forcing the closure of McConnells Highway. Gunsmoke Rd was also closed at Hawley Rd, about 12 miles west southwest of Rock Hill. This was due to flooding along Conrad Creek. Saluda Rd was also closed at highway 324, likely due to flooding along Stony Fork.

Although most of the flooding was of the urban/poor drainage variety, at least two creeks were involved, including Whitner and Cox Creeks. Affected roads included Southwood St (due to Whitner Creek) and Rantowles Rd (due to Cox Creek), where water entered some homes. At least a half dozen additional streets were closed in the city due to urban flooding. These included Susan St and Booker St, which were covered by as much as 3 feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of rain, sleet, and snow developed across the extreme northern part of South Carolina around sunrise, and gradually spread east along the I-85 corridor throughout the morning. Measurable snowfall occurred in areas along/north of the North Carolina border, where as much as 3 inches fell. Light accumulations of sleet occurred in areas further south. Although the precipitation changed to mainly rain during the afternoon, temperatures that hovered right around freezing allowed for some light ice accretion to occur on elevated surfaces.

Supercell thunderstorms moved across the lower midlands and produced large tornadoes.  Other storms in the central midlands produced large hail and microburst wind damage.  The largest tornado tracked across Aiken county along highway 278.  There was one indirect fatality and around a dozen people were sent to the hospital. Several homes were destroyed and many had moderate to severe damage.  Widespread trees and powerlines were also down in Aiken county.  Total damage estimate was 6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado that tracked across Burke county GA, jumped the river and briefly touched down in Barnwell county near the Savannah River site taking down trees.

Many reports of flooding in streets and in yards around the city.  Some vehicles are inundated. There were no injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling in the early morning hours, with several spotters and the public reporting nearly two inches of snow on grassy surfaces. Most of the main roadways were not affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the area during the evening and produced a few severe thunderstorms in Orangeburg and Clarendon counties.  One tornado was confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two veteran spotters photographed and video taped the tornado as it touched down in the woods off of Bill Davis road and traveled northeast just east of Davis Station.  Numerous trees were down but there was no structural damage as no homes or buildings were in its path.  Photo coutesy of Gerard V. Jebaily, KI4LJT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds downed a few trees over Upstate South Carolina during the 7th and 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of gusty winds and saturated ground due to recent heavy rainfall caused a large tree to fall on a home on highway 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds downed a few trees over Upstate South Carolina during the 7th and 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree, apparently weakened due to strong winds from the previous day and a wet ground, fell on a home in the Duncan Park area, causing quite a bit of damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms produced large hail over the northeastern Upstate of South Carolina.

Supercell thunderstorms moved into the western upstate in the early evening hours. As the evening progressed, the supercells tended to evolve into a series of bow echoes. Tornadoes were spawned by the supercells and the resultant bow echoes. There was also quite a bit of large hail and straight-line wind damage in places as well. The most significant damage occurred in Greenwood and Abbeville Counties, though tornadoes also caused damage in parts of Anderson and Union Counties as well.

An area of low pressure and associated cold front pushed through the southeast where kinematic profiles and anomalously cold air aloft existed. This lead to the development of strong storms over Allendale county overnight on April 10, 2009.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Survey Team determined a tornado touched down along Roberts Road 3.5 miles northeast of Martin, South Carolina at 1230 AM local time Saturday morning and traveled 5.6 miles before lifting. Initially the tornado uprooted or snapped numerous trees with one tree falling on a mobile home and damaging the roof of the structure. The under-skirting of the mobile home was peeled back by the winds and a couple windows were blown out. Additionally, several cars sustained damage by falling trees outside of the mobile home. Another couple homes along Roberts Road received minor rood damage and a couple carports were slightly damaged as well. The tornado continued to nearly parallel Miller Creek Road downing numerous trees and power lines. The maximum width of the tornado was approximately 500 yards near the intersection of Camp Jackson Road and Miller Creek Road. The tornado lifted 2.3 miles south southeast of Kline, South Carolina just past the intersection of Appleton Road and Barnwell Highway /US Highway 278/ at 1239 AM local time, but not before damaging the roof and cinder-block walls of a home, downing numerous trees and completely destroying the historic Cave Methodist Church. The church was wiped completely off its foundation. Several grave stones surrounding the church were overturned or knocked down. An 18 wheel truck was overturned a couple hundred feet from the Cave Methodist Church with a tree falling on it and causing further damage. In total, 4 homes, 1 mobile home, 1 church and 5 vehicles received damage as a result of the tornado. In addition, hundreds of acres of timber was damaged or destroyed. The tornado did not result in any injuries or fatalities. The National Weather Service Damage Survey Team determined the strength of the tornado to be an EF2 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale with winds estimated between 120 and 130 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms moved into the western upstate in the early evening hours. As the evening progressed, the supercells tended to evolve into a series of bow echoes. Tornadoes were spawned by the supercells and the resultant bow echoes. There was also quite a bit of large hail and straight-line wind damage in places as well. The most significant damage occurred in Greenwood and Abbeville Counties, though tornadoes also caused damage in parts of Anderson and Union Counties as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second tornado path was found a couple of miles north of the main tornado track through Greenwood. This tornado began on Karlie Ct just off Laurel Rd. Once again, numerous trees were uprooted or snapped off. However, more structural damage was observed along this path. Several homes received minor to moderate roof damage. Siding and gutters were blown off a few homes as well. A home near the end of the path received the most significant, damage, with part of the roof completely blown off and and the collapse of an adjacent exterior wall. A storage trailer was also blown over in this area. Also, a tree fell across a train track on Emerald Rd, causing a train to derail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and associated cold front pushed through the southeast where kinematic profiles and anomalously cold air aloft existed. This lead to the development of strong storms over Allendale county overnight on April 10, 2009.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team determined that a tornado touched down 2.1 miles north-northwest of Martin, South Carolina at 1226 AM Saturday Morning. The tornado traveled approximately 1.2 miles before lifting 1.8 miles north-northwest of Martin at approximately 1228 AM. There was significant tree damage throughout the path of the tornado, but no structures were damaged. The tornado did not result in any injuries or fatalities. The National Weather Service Damage Survey Team determined the strength of the tornado to be an EF1 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale with winds estimated to be around 100 miles per hour. The maximum damage width was 300 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and associated cold front pushed through the southeast where kinematic profiles and anomalously cold air aloft existed. This lead to the development of strong storms over Allendale county overnight on April 10, 2009.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Damage Survey Team determined tree damage found 2.4 miles northeast of Millet, South Carolina was caused by a thunderstorm microburst. Peak winds were estimated to be around 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported at the White Hall subdivision in North Charleston, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms moved into the western upstate in the early evening hours. As the evening progressed, the supercells tended to evolve into a series of bow echoes. Tornadoes were spawned by the supercells and the resultant bow echoes. There was also quite a bit of large hail and straight-line wind damage in places as well. The most significant damage occurred in Greenwood and Abbeville Counties, though tornadoes also caused damage in parts of Anderson and Union Counties as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS survey found an extensive area of damage in the city of Abbeville consistent with a strong EF2 tornado. The tornado began near the intersection of highway 71 and Rock Hill Rd before moving east/southeast through the city of Abbeville. The main areas affected in the city were Church Street, McGowan, Ave, Main St, and especially Secession Ave. The roofs were blown off about a half dozen frame homes in the city, while numerous mobile homes were heavily damaged or destroyed. A few other office buildings and businesses received major roof damage in this area. Numerous trees were snapped off or uprooted along the path. One tree fell through a home, injuring the man inside. Another man caught outside received head injuries from flying debris. The tornado lifted about a mile east/southeast of the city. The tornado was produced by a supercell embedded in a line of rapidly moving storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow during the early evening across the northwest Piedmont of South Carolina. Snow became heavy at times throughout the evening, and up to 4 inches had accumulated across the area by 10 pm. Snow, heavy at times and accompanied by occasional lightning, continued into the late evening and early overnight. By the time the snow tapered off, accumulations of 3-6 inches were common across the area. The heavy wet snow, combined with gusty winds, caused quite a few trees and power lines to fall, resulting in numerous power outages. Some structures received minor to moderate roof damage due to the weight of the snow. Some customers were without power for several days. Numerous traffic accidents also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire occurred near the community of Sandy Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire broke out 3 miles north of the residential village of Sandy Island. A total of 1100 acres burned before the fire was contained. No structures or residential areas were threatened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry windy conditions aided a large wildfire that burned a good portion of the Barefoot Resort.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began on Woodland Drive, 8 miles east of Conway near Hwy 90. The fire spread to the east, and eventually to the northeast, briefly jumping Hwy 31 and also Hwy 22. The South Carolina governor declared a state of emergency for Horry County on April 23rd, activating more personnel and agencies to deal with the fire. A total of 19600 acres burned before the fire was contained on April 28th. Approximately 2500 people were evacuated, and a total of 76 homes were destroyed and at least another 100 damaged, mostly in the Barefoot Resort area in North Myrtle Beach. Dense smoke affected the region for about a week. Of the 40 million total in damages 25 million was attributed to structures and 15 million estimated for woodland loss by SC forestry.

The Westbrook and Martins Grove neighborhoods were hit the hardest. Several homes sustained some damage to vinyl siding and a couple homes had roof damage. There were numerous large limbs down but also several trees that were snapped off or up rooted. Limbs and trees fell on a few houses as well as one car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms moved into the western upstate in the early evening hours. As the evening progressed, the supercells tended to evolve into a series of bow echoes. Tornadoes were spawned by the supercells and the resultant bow echoes. There was also quite a bit of large hail and straight-line wind damage in places as well. The most significant damage occurred in Greenwood and Abbeville Counties, though tornadoes also caused damage in parts of Anderson and Union Counties as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down across much of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Damage Survey Team evaluated damage which occurred at Charleston Southern University, near College Park, South Carolina.  The survey teams findings included, three large trees and numerous large tree limbs were blown down and an air conditioning unit sustained structural damage.  The damage path was estimated to be a half mile long and a quarter mile wide.  Damage was consistent with a downburst with winds estimated to be between 60 and 70 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported at the intersection of highway 29 and Oneal St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong heating allowed thunderstorms to develop along and ahead of a weak cold front. These thunderstorms intensified as an upper level jet streak impinged on the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong heating allowed thunderstorms to develop along and ahead of a weak cold front. These thunderstorms intensified as an upper level jet streak impinged on the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong heating allowed thunderstorms to develop along and ahead of a weak cold front. These thunderstorms intensified as an upper level jet streak impinged on the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey concluded a tornado initially touched down near the intersection of Highway 378 and Marigold Road causing damage to some trees. One tree fell onto a single wide trailer along Marigold Road and ripped part of the roof off. The tornado caused sporadic tree damage before causing significant damage to an abandoned barn along Marigold Road. The top of the barn was sheared back into a small nearby field. There were also about a dozen pine trees snapped in the vicinity of the barn. The tornado moved north across Hampton Road, near the intersection of Marigold Road and dissipated. The tornado was classified as an EF-0 with winds estimated to 80 mph. The path length was 1.12 miles and the maximum path width was 40 yards. There were no injuries or fatalities. The Horry County Emergency Manager estimated the monetary damage at $13,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong heating allowed thunderstorms to develop along and ahead of a weak cold front. These thunderstorms intensified as an upper level jet streak impinged on the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey found evidence of straight-line wind damage along a small portion of Hampton Road. The damage was located just to the east of where an EF-0 tornado dissipated. A two story house sustained moderate roof damage when a tree fell onto the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm collided with the seabreeze and outflow from a previous thunderstorm and briefly pulsed to severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down at the intersection of U.S. 701 and Exodus Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several supercell thunderstorms affected the western Carolinas during the afternoon and evening hours, producing very large hail, wind damage and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS survey found an area of extensive straight line wind damage. The damage began near Gray Court, but winds intensified significantly along West Main St in the city of Laurens, mainly in areas from Todd Ave to Laurens High School. Hundreds of trees were snapped off or uprooted. A 29-year-old woman was killed on highway 321 south of the city when a tree fell on her vehicle. Numerous trees fell on homes, causing roof damage. Other trees fell on and destroyed automobiles and outbuildings. For the most part, damage caused directly by the wind was minor. However, a detached garage at the intersection of highway 76 and Stagecoach Rd had its roof blown off, causing two of the walls to collapse. Another small structure was destroyed on LC Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather affected western South Carolina as a line of storms moved across much of the region. Other severe storms also developed ahead of the line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of the metal roof was blown off of a car dealership and numerous trees were blown down around Pickens. Three trees were also blown down onto highway 183 3.5 miles east of Pickens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough interacted with an upper level shortwave trough resulting in upstream thunderstorm development. As these thunderstorms moved to the southeast, they intensified to severe levels as they interacted with the sea breeze boundary pushing progressively inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in the Woodwind Mobile Home Park located adjacent to  SC-544. Two homes were damaged by fallen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable environment existed ahead of an approaching cold front. Convection initiated in the wake of a departing meso-scale convective vortex during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down along Highway 52. The report was relayed through the Williamsburg County 911 Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough, low level convergence, and high precipitable water produced heavy rain, causing flash flooding in Georgetown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten people stranded at city hall were rescued in a jon boat.  Six people were rescued from a flooded car at the intersection of Highmarket and Cannon Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rain fell between the 18th and 22nd across the northwest corner of South Carolina, mostly of over Oconee and western Pickens Counties. The highest rainfall total for the period was 15.15 inches, reported by a cooperative observer at the Walhalla Fish Hatchery in northwest Oconee County. Most of the flooding occurred on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Creeks flooded several roads in the northern part of the county, including Cassidy Bridge Rd and Whetstone Rd. Several roads were also flooded in Walhalla, including Andover Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rain fell between the 18th and 22nd across the northwest corner of South Carolina, mostly of over Oconee and western Pickens Counties. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The very heavy rainfall that caused flash flooding early in the day ended by early  afternoon. However, periods of moderate to heavy rain caused flooding to persist in areas from Long Creek to Walhalla through the afternoon. At the height of the event, 33 roads were closed in this area. In addition to the flooding, quite a few trees fell in the saturated soil.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS at Marion County Airport measured a wind gust to 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front. A few became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.

A very moist and unstable air mass was in place across the area through the afternoon and evening hours. An area of low pressure aloft moved by to our north. The southern fringes of the coolest temperatures aloft and strongest mid-level winds affected the area through the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey concluded a microburst with winds to 65 mph ripped the roof off of a single wide mobile home and partially tore the siding off of a manufactured home. Ten large trees were snapped or uprooted. The damage occurred from near Violet Road to Lynchburg Road. The path length was 230 yards. The maximum path width was 0.5 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a trough of low pressure and the inland propagating sea breeze, resulted in the development of numerous thunderstorms across southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways reported 3 trees down in Reevesville, South Carolina. One tree was reported down along Highway 78, another on Saint Mark Bowman Road, and another on Wire Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance in combination with the sea breeze in a highly unstable air mass, resulted in scattered thunderstorm development across southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Beaufort County Emergency Manager reported that an older building had a portion of its roof blown off by strong thunderstorm winds in Lemon Island, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe convection developed along a lee side trough across the Midlands of South Carolina and central Georgia, then shifted east into the forecast area during the afternoon and evening as a potent upper level disturbance crossed the area. This convection interacted with the sea breeze (which happened to be pinched rather close to the coast) allowing for additional intensification closer to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported penny size hail 5 miles east-southeast of Bonneau, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe convection developed along a lee side trough across the Midlands of South Carolina and central Georgia, then shifted east into the forecast area during the afternoon and evening as a potent upper level disturbance crossed the area. This convection interacted with the sea breeze (which happened to be pinched rather close to the coast) allowing for additional intensification closer to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Allendale County Emergency Manager reported several trees down along Highway 278, one of which fell on a shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow line of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front and moved rapidly across western South Carolina during the evening hours of the 11th. Several reports of straight line wind damage were received in association with the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down near the intersection of Bush Rd and highway 55.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms moved into the western upstate in the early evening hours. As the evening progressed, the supercells tended to evolve into a series of bow echoes. Tornadoes were spawned by the supercells and the resultant bow echoes. There was also quite a bit of large hail and straight-line wind damage in places as well. The most significant damage occurred in Greenwood and Abbeville Counties, though tornadoes also caused damage in parts of Anderson and Union Counties as well.
A strong upper level trough and an afternoon seabreeze promoted strong uplift and the development of thunderstorms in a highly unstable environment over southeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An electric company reported 1 tree down on Woodsville Road near Pineland, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cluster thunderstorms produced large hail and strong winds over portions of the Midlands.  A supercell moved in from the foothills across the north Midlands and produced a couple of small tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT reported several trees down on SC Hwys 19, 37, and 53.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak and dissipating back door cold front interacted with the sea breeze front and became the focus for afternoon and evening thunderstorms. A very unstable airmass coupled with unseasonably cool temperatures aloft produced a widespread outbreak of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of pennies was reported.
